data.title: EWZS: Brazilian Small Caps Safest Emerging Market Play
data.description:
Brazil Stocks Bottoming Out

Brazilian small-cap stocks have taken a beating in the past six months and may be headed for a further decline amid the new trade war tensions that kicked off this week.

Brazil is heavily exposed to the United States and China but, as past cycles have shown, it may be able to benefit from this cycle in some ways. If commodity prices take off, Brazil is also positioned to benefit as a significant commodity exporter. It is best to not overlook Brazil, which is currently one of the cheapest emerging markets.

Growth will likely come in a bit slower in 2025 and 2026, but the market already appears to be priced for the worst at the moment. Despite being a top 5 MSCI Emerging Markets constituent, the market still trades at a 30% discount to this index.

The iShares MSCI Brazil Small-Cap ETF (NASDAQ:EWZS) has underperformed general Brazilian ETFs but has also outperformed its small-cap peer ETF. 2025 looks like a good time to continue accumulating, especially after any trade war related sell-offs. The market already appears priced for the worse.

Economic Outlook

Brazil's economic growth will likely fall below 3% this year, below its historical average, due to global economic tensions. After the next few months, Brazil will be positioned to consider smaller rate cuts to help create stronger growth in 2026.

Growth is on a downturn: Brazil's average growth has been around 3% during the past three years, and recently grew by 3.4% last year. However, the combination of higher interest rates, trade war risks, and other global macro tensions will likely bring its economic growth below 3% in the next two years.

Weak Currency and Inflation: The combination of the country's weaker currency and rebound in inflation forced the Central Bank to reverse its policy and begin hiking rates again towards the end of 2024.

Rate Cut Outlook: Brazil's central bank has noted that it may step in to cut interest rates during the second half of this year. Even though inflation has crept up this year, it still has remained below 5.1% every month for the last 12 months. 2026 may be much more favorable, according to projections from BCB.

China and US Impact: Following the previous trade war, there was a significant uptick in Brazil's exports to China during the following five months. In the long run, its exports to China have nearly doubled since 2017.

Brazil's Exports to China

Similarly, Brazil's exports to the United States have also risen steadily since 2017. Given that Trump has been willing to discuss free trade agreements with major partners, like Argentina, for example, Brazil could come out ahead here by staying close to China and the United States this year. These markets account for nearly 40% of its exports though, so any economic shocks in these markets would be detrimental for Brazil.

Value Investing in Small Caps

The iShares MSCI Brazil Small-Cap ETF invests in 72 companies and charges a 0.59% management fee. This ETF is a stellar value vehicle that trades at a substantial discount to the MSCI Brazil Index. It is well diversified, with the top 10 holdings only accounting for 34% of its assets.

This ETF trades at around 7.6x earnings and trades slightly below book value. The assets of this ETF currently exceed $100 million, so there is not a strong risk of delisting, as seen with other smaller ETFs with AUM below $40 million. Moreover, Brazil is still the 5th largest MSCI EM Index constituent and also accounts for more than 60% of the MSCI Emerging Market Latin America Index.

MSCI EM Latin America Index

Sector Exposure: Since I last covered this ETF in 2023, some of the sector weights have changed. The consumer themes weighting rose by around 3 percentage points, industrials dropped by over 10 pp, and financials rose by around 11 pp.

This looks like a very favorable way to approach the market, especially since this ETF trades at a substantial discount to Brazilian large caps. While it has underperformed traditional Brazil ETFs, this ETF has outperformed the VanEck Brazil Small-Cap ETF (BRF) by around 15 percentage points in the last five years.

As seen in this chart, this ETF was a major outperformer following 2020 when emerging market stocks bottomed out, so this trend could repeat when emerging markets rally again.

Risks and Thoughts to Note

The next couple of months will be bumpy for emerging markets, so finding the perfect entry point is nearly impossible.

Emerging markets, particularly China, have been hit harder than the S&P 500 in the past five days.

Trade War: We are certainly not out of the clear in terms of tariffs, as tensions have only been escalating recently with US partners like China and Canada. Brazil's export structure is not very diversified, as China and the USA alone account for circa 40% of its total exports.

Inflation: A lot of inflation projections were made with trade war risks in mind, but may have underestimated the current tensions. Brazil is less exposed because it only faces the minimum tariff charge. However, other inflationary risks, such as tensions in the Middle East, may surprise to the upside this year. Brazil's Central Bank has taken a very cautious approach to rate cuts, even with inflation improving in 2024, and will likely hold back for a couple more months at least.

Peers: EM Latin American markets are also very cheap, only trading at a slight premium to Brazil. If free trade agreements go through with Argentina and the USA, this market could absorb capital flows. Other markets like Colombia are also extremely undervalued.

Other Risks: Brazil's currency was one of the worst performing major currencies in 2024, declining by around 20%. The country's external debt levels are still relatively high, as the country has had to take on a lot of new debt since Covid.

Brazil's External Debt

Valuation Appeal: The main appeal of Brazil is also related to the lack of compelling narratives in other emerging markets. Other larger Asian emerging markets also stand to suffer from trade war risks, yet these markets are much more expensive. The MSCI Asia AC Index trades at 15x earnings, a notable premium to Brazil and other South American markets.

At the moment, Brazil can be characterized as a market with acceptable risks and high return potential. It is not directly exposed like China yet is priced at a steal. In particular, this ETF trades at a significant discount to the broader market in Brazil.

It makes sense to take advantage of any pullbacks in Q2 2025 to gain exposure to Brazil. Investors will be hard-pressed to find another emerging market with a better risk reward potential.

data.title: Ultra High Conviction: My #1 Defensive Place To Invest Right Now
data.description:
Introduction

Markets are in turmoil.

In the past two weeks, we had:

  • The S&P 500's third-best day since World War II, as a result of a 90-day tariff pause for all nations except China.
  • The largest Cboe Volatility Index decline in history, as it fell by more than 30% on the same day the market had its third-best day.
  • The market's fifth-worst two-day drop in early April, when 'Liberation Day' kicked off a new wave of tariffs.
  • The Dow Jones had its biggest four-day losing streak ever. However, this needs to be taken with a grain of salt, as this is measured in points instead of percentage points. But still, it sounds catchy.
  • The worst week in years was followed by the best week since 2023.

Things have become so volatile that it's almost impossible for me to write long articles, as the moment I'm halfway, some new headline drops that cause the market to move a few percent.

However, the worst thing is that markets have started to exhibit a lot of fear.

By that, I mean that 'normal' trading patterns have broken down.

For example, we're seeing both one of the steepest declines in the dollar index and one of the steepest increases in longer-term bond yields.

The surge in rates happens despite a highly favorable inflation number on Thursday and the positive 90-day tariff pause news. It also did not keep the dollar from plummeting, which triggered another rally in the price of gold.

Although I disagree with everyone who will make the case that America is losing its reserve currency status, I agree with people who point out that all of this screams elevated instability:

Treasuries and the dollar get their strength from “the world’s perception of the competence of American fiscal and monetary management and the solidity of American political and financial institutions,” said Jim Grant, founder of Grant’s Interest Rate Observer, a widely followed financial newsletter. “Possibly, the world is reconsidering.” - Bloomberg

In fact, U.S. treasuries had their worst performance relative to German bunds in history, as the chart below shows. That's quite something, as Germany itself is dealing with structural issues and is heavily reliant on government spending.

And then there's oil, which is has fallen below $60, as the market is pricing in much slower economic growth on a global scale, as globalization was a major driver of international prosperity - even if it was a net negative for industrial regions like the American Rust Belt or the German Ruhrgebiet, among many others.

Moreover, earnings estimates are coming down. Even before 'Liberation Day,' earnings estimates for the S&P 500 were adjusted at a slightly above-average rate, as FactSet data shows.

During the first quarter, analysts lowered EPS estimates by a larger margin compared to the three most recent averages. The Q1 bottom-up EPS estimate (which is an aggregation of the median EPS estimates for Q1 for all the companies in the index) decreased by 4.2% (to $60.23 from $62.89) from December 31 to March 31. - FactSet

As earnings season is about to shift into a higher gear in the upcoming two weeks, we'll get a lot more data and valuable comments from executives who will comment on tariffs, consumer sentiment, inflation, artificial intelligence, and everything else you can think of.

Having said all of this, people asked me what my favorite defensive industry is for (dividend) investors, in light of global uncertainty.

Like all "if you could pick just one" questions, it took me a few days to figure it out until I realized my favorite defensive industry is part of a cyclical sector.

Hence, in the second part, I will tell you my favorite industry for the next few years and the three stocks that stand out to me.

Now, let's dive in!

It's All About One Word & One Place That Benefits

"Uncertainty."

So far, that's the theme of 2025, as we are dealing with a mix of tariff fears, economic growth worries, inflation uncertainty, and other factors.

To make matters worse, all of these factors are somewhat related and influencing each other, as without sticky inflation and somewhat 'unfortunate choices' from the prior administration, the U.S. would not have to refinance trillions in debt at what are currently very unfavorable rates.

In light of the many challenges, I wrote articles like "Buckle Up Buttercup [...]" in December of 2024, as I believe the next few years are not going to be as comfortable as the past few years, which had rather low volatility and elevated returns, as the JPMorgan chart below shows quite well (as of March 31).

With that said, I had many sectors/industries in mind.

  • Utilities are defensive, they enjoy secular growth from AI-related power demand, they tend to have decent dividends, and overall, healthy balance sheets. However, to me, they are too boring. For income, they may be interesting, but that's it.
  • Real estate is one of my favorite sectors, as it has been beaten down by rising rates. A lot of REITs offer terrific dividends and defensive business models, as the 'big guys' have all kept strong occupancy rates in periods like the Great Financial Crisis. However, due to interest rate risks, it's not my number on sector.
  • I also thought of making energy the star of this article. It's one of the cheapest places to put money due to the decline in oil prices. It is also home to some very efficient companies with low breakeven prices and consistent dividend growth (even during recessions). However, due to the cyclical nature of these stocks and elevated volatility, they are not number one. Also, they are not defensive, which is the number one criterion for this article.
  • Consumer staples are another area I like. Even the strongest players like PepsiCo (PEP), which I own, have suffered due to fears of declining pricing power. However, as much as I like this industry, the risks of prolonged uncertainty and tariff fears keep it from becoming my number one sector.

As we can see below, the three defensive sectors I just mentioned all outperformed the S&P 500 year-to-date (excluding dividends). Energy (not defensive) started underperforming the market due to OPEC's decision to increase output, which could not have come at a worse time.

While I like various stocks in the sectors I just mentioned, this article is about a different sector, or at least about a quite unique industry in a cyclical sector.

I'm talking about the financial exchange & data industry, which accounts for roughly half of the iShares U.S. Broker-Dealers & Securities Exchange ETF (IAI).

As we can see below, the IAI has underperformed the S&P 500 this year, after outperforming the market over the past ten years (256% vs. 197%, including dividends). This makes sense, as the other half of the ETF consists of investment banks, which are hyper cyclical.

The industry I am talking about is much stronger.

Stock exchange operators own the plumbing of the (global) financial system. They operate the exchanges we need to trade, which allows them to make money on every trade we execute, the more, the better.

On top of that, there are other (related) factors that make them great:

  • Higher demand for hedging products: During volatile markets, investors and institutions use derivatives like options and futures to manage risk. The higher the uncertainty, the more corporations and other investors tend to use hedging products.
  • Elevated market data sales: Volatile periods tend to draw more attention to real-time market data, as traders and institutions need insights, which leads to higher subscription and access fees for exchange-provided data. Moreover, the more people trade, the more data exchange companies can generate. This means the bigger they become, the more 'size' becomes a competitive advantage.
  • Clearing and settlement activity: Higher trading during periods of volatility increases the need for clearing services, which ensure trades are completed. Without reliable trade execution, we would run into serious issues. While recent volatility wasn't fun, even when markets crashed more than 6% in a single day, the 'system' worked.
  • Scalability: Because exchanges are almost entirely electronic (we don't have to fly to Chicago to bet on corn futures), it is very easy for these companies to scale operations, which often comes with very high margin benefits and inflation protection. It also helps that inflation surges often come with higher volatility.

Simply put, the higher the volatility and uncertainty, the more these companies benefit.

The 3 Companies Worth Buying & Watching

There are three stock exchange operators that dominate the landscape:

  • CME Group (CME)
  • Intercontinental Exchange (ICE)
  • Cboe Global Markets (CBOE)

There are also smaller players, including Coinbase Global (COIN) for crypto and MarketAxess Holdings (MKTX), which is an emerging player for bond futures.

However, for now, I focus on the three biggest players, which I prefer anyway, as they have less 'size' and 'niche' risks like COIN and MKTX.

The interesting thing is that each of these companies brings something very special to the table.

CME Group is a pure-play exchange operator and the largest derivatives marketplace on the face of the earth, with portfolio exchanges like the Chicago Mercantile Exchange (where it got its name), Chicago Board of Trade, NYMEX, and COMEX.

On its exchanges, investors trade some of the most liquid assets, including the most liquid futures in the world, which is the S&P 500 e-mini. It also offers a wide range of agricultural, interest rate, energy, metal, and forex futures and options, providing investors and corporate players with almost everything they need to get the job done.

Additionally, it is consistently adding new products, including Bitcoin futures, lithium, and other niche futures for very specific purposes.

This venture into new areas is a great way to benefit from emerging trends without having to invest a substantial amount, which is the asset-light benefit I just briefly mentioned.

With regard to the current environment, in 1Q25 (before the current turmoil started), average daily volumes were up 13%, which is a terrific result, likely followed by a much higher number for the second quarter.

Intercontinental Exchange is different. The company, which owns the New York Stock Exchange, has made the decision to diversify. Currently, it generates roughly half of its money from exchange operations, with most revenue coming from energy and financial futures. These futures include targeted financial products for specific purposes.

For example, it has Midland WTI futures for Permian (Midland) oil production, Brent Crude futures, European natural gas futures (including LNG), Dubai Murban futures, and many other products. Since 2019, this energy business has grown by 14% per year.

Meanwhile, the other half of its revenue comes from mortgage technologies and fixed income/data services. Due to aggressive M&A, the company now owns a big part of the mortgage 'supply chain', which includes real estate data tracking, lead generation, underwriting, closing, loan trading, and loan servicing, among many other things.

As a result, despite being an exchange operator, roughly half of its revenue is recurring, which adds a layer of safety. And while its mortgage business is cyclical, it has strong secular growth due to mortgage process modernization.

Then there's Cboe.

Cboe Global Markets operates as an exchange for options, equities, futures, and foreign exchange, with a core focus on the Chicago Board Options Exchange, which is where it got its name.

Unlike CME and ICE, its business model revolves around supporting trading in equity index options (like SPX and VIX), single-stock options, and exchange-traded products.

Revenue streams include transaction fees, clearing fees, market data subscriptions, and proprietary index licensing. This includes the Cboe Volatility Index, which I have brought up almost daily in recent weeks.

Cboe also operates equities exchanges in the U.S., Europe, and Canada, diversifying its footprint while benefiting from strong international demand for American financial equities.

Or to put it differently, Cboe is an option-focused company with growth opportunities in areas that have gone overlooked by many other players in recent years.

Especially after the pandemic, when people became more speculative, Cboe hit it out of the park, supported by new products like 0DTE (same-day expiration) options.

Personally, I bought CME Group because I loved its pure-play futures/options business model and its special dividends.

With regard to dividends, both ICE and CBOE pay steadily rising quarterly dividends.

ICE, which yields 1.2%, has a payout ratio of just 30% and a five-year CAGR of 10.2%. It has grown its dividend every year for more than a decade, supported by aggressive buybacks in years without M&A-related deleveraging.

Meanwhile, CBOE shares also yield 1.2% with a payout ratio of 27% and a five-year CAGR of 12%. The company has hiked its dividend every single year for 14 years. Now, it has a bigger focus on organic growth (without M&A), which will likely support elevated dividend growth going forward.

CME Group also pays four quarterly dividends.

However, it also pays one annual special dividend, which usually pushes the total cash payout ratio to 100%. CME simply doesn't need a lot of cash to run the business. And, as it has a double A-range credit rating, it does not need to take care of its balance sheet.

So, almost every penny of free cash flow goes to shareholders.

Currently, I estimate that the company will pay close to 5% on an annualized basis. The base dividend accounts for roughly half of that number. In February, the base dividend was hiked by 8.7%.

Having said all of this, over the past ten years, an equal-weight basket of these three stocks has returned 16.9% per year, beating the 13.4% annual return of the S&P 500 by a very decent margin.

Even better, the standard deviation was barely higher at 16.7%, which is a fantastic accomplishment, as we are comparing just three stocks to a diversified portfolio of 500 stocks.

Hence, the risk-adjusted return of these three stocks was much better than the market, as the Sharpe and Sortino Ratios show.

Also, and this matters most, the three stocks ignored the recent sell-off, as we can see below.

So, what about their valuations?

Let's start with the bad news.

Cboe isn't cheap anymore. Currently, it trades at 24x earnings. While that's just slightly above its long-term average of 23.0x, it would require more than 8% annual EPS growth to produce an annual return of 10% or more.

While current expectations are for 8% EPS growth in 2025 and 2026 (FactSet), it's not a perfect risk/reward, especially because the current sell-off has produced some great bargains in other areas.

In my view, Cboe is a Hold.

ICE is a Buy.

The company trades at 24.8x earnings, in line with its long-term average. Analysts expect 11-12% annual EPS growth. Especially if rates come down a bit, I think ICE could benefit from long-term elevated volatility and secular growth in mortgages.

CME Group is a Buy as well.

Currently, the stock trades at 24.9x earnings. Its long-term average is 27.1x. While that may seem elevated, please note that it has the fourth-highest profit margins of the S&P 500 and a 100% payout.

These are high-quality earnings, as a lot of it ends up in shareholders' pockets instead of reinvestments.

When adding mid-single-digit annual EPS growth (I believe it will be much higher), we get an outlook of more than 12% annual total returns, similar to ICE.

All things considered, I love the stock exchange industry.

I own CME, and I'm looking to buy ICE as well.

Going forward, I truly believe these are the perfect assets to own for prolonged volatility and uncertainty.

If CME comes down a bit, I'll substantially expand my investment, likely making it one of my largest holdings.

Takeaway

Markets are on edge, with wild swings in stocks, bonds, oil, and currencies that make it hard to write an article before the next headline pops up.

While many defensive sectors like utilities, real estate, and staples have their benefits, I have realized my favorite defensive industry right now isn't in a defensive sector at all.

It's the financial exchanges industry, which includes companies like CME, ICE, and CBOE.

These are the behind-the-scenes operators of the global financial system, and the more uncertainty we face, the more they tend to thrive.

Higher volatility means more trading, more demand for data, and more hedging activity, which feeds directly into their income statements.

In a world where the only constant seems to be elevated uncertainty, these scalable, margin-rich businesses look like great places to be, with consistently rising income on top.

I don't think it gets much better than this, especially as I expect volatility to remain above-average on a longer-term basis.

Risks To My Thesis

The biggest risk to my thesis is a sudden drop in market volatility. If uncertainty fades and trading volumes decline, demand for derivatives and market data could weaken.

If we were to get a longer-term market rebound, these stocks could temporarily underperform.

Also, general recession and market risks apply. While these three stocks have outperformed the market, a steep bear market and/or recession can cause (temporary) capital losses.

data.title: Duolingo: A Growth Play Amid Tariff Uncertainty
data.description:
Since 2023, the stock price of Duolingo (NASDAQ:DUOL) has soared, achieving a performance of 327%. In FY 2023 and FY 2024, the company achieved GAAP profitability and revenues continued to grow at an accelerated level.

When Duolingo went public in 2020, the financials looked completely different, with revenues of $161.7 million and a net income loss of $-158 million. Three years later, things drastically changed, and revenues multiplied by more than four at $748.0 million, while the company achieved a net income of $88.6 million.

Duolingo: A Game-Like App To Learn Languages

Duolingo is the most downloaded educational app in the world. Users can learn more than 40 languages through game-like lessons for free. From there, the app offers a premium version named Super Duolingo, which removes ads, provides offline access, and gives the ability to play unlimited. At the same time, the company has introduced other features, such as math and music learning, to expand its total addressable market.

Duolingo's freemium model has attracted user growth at a fast CAGR of 55% since 2021, reaching 40.5 million daily active users. This growth is due to a combination of strong word of mouth and viral marketing campaigns, such as its 5-second Super Bowl ad, Duolingo on Ice, and "Owl Game" partnership with Netflix.

In 2023, the company introduced Duolingo Max, which is an enhanced premium version of Super Duolingo, giving users access to features such as Explain My Answer, Roleplay Conversations, and Video Call with Lily. This higher-tier version is priced in the US at $139.99 per year, compared to the $79.99 of the classical version.

This video call feature will combat Duolingo's historical challenge of speaking. At the same time, it has certain advantages over free GenAI tools. For example, Lily can memorize previous conversations and proactively call you as if it were a friend. This is certainly a valuable feature for practicing a language, and diminishes competitor Tandem's comparable advantage of matching strangers to practice languages.

2020 2021 2022 2023 2024
% Change 1.8% 1.6% 0.5% 0.5%
Penetration Rate 4.4% 6.2% 7.8% 8.3% 8.8%

Source: DUOL IR

There are currently 9.5 million paid users of Super Duolingo and Duolingo Max, implying a paid subscriber penetration of 8.8%. In Q4 2020, the penetration rate was just 4.4% of the monthly active users, doubling in five years. Nonetheless, as exhibited above, the penetration growth has drastically reduced in 2024 and 2023, both growing at 0.5% percentage points, which is much lower than the penetration growth achieved during the pandemic years.

AI Tailwind Could Become a Headwind

With the help of artificial intelligence, the content creation team of Duolingo has been able to increase considerably the course units published each year, as displayed above. More content enhances the user experience, especially in languages that aren't too popular. Nevertheless, this could imply that with AI, competitors could also create great content with the quality level of Duolingo more easily, therefore intensifying competition and cutting Duolingo's edge over the years.

In 2025, Duolingo plans to make significant investments in AI for its top-tier version, Duolingo Max. The company expects gross margins to contract due to the expensive technological costs, but over time, they believe these marginal costs will start to trend lower.

"In 2025, we expect a temporary 170 basis point year over year impact on gross margin, primarily due to Max. In the first half of the year there'll be roughly a 300 basis point year over year impact as we prioritize rapid product innovation to drive max adoption. We expect margins to improve in the second half of the year as we work to improve AI costs." Matt Skaruppa CFO.

Valuation: Shares are Undervalued

As a company that went public while having a net loss and later became profitable, reaching a net income margin of 11.84%, the PE ratio of Duolingo has been contracting significantly throughout the years. At the same time, the PE divided by its one-year expected growth (PEG ratio) is at 0.36x, implying that the stock is undervalued.

In addition, based on a DCF model that takes the company's 2025 revenue growth guidance and sequentially reduces the growth, the shares have an intrinsic valuation of $359. Comparing this to the actual price of approximately $310, the shares are undervalued and have an upside potential of 15.7%.

Takeaway & Stock Rating

Duolingo's core KPIs have been constantly growing throughout the years, and there is no reason to believe that this company won't continue experiencing these high growth rates, even more so with the enhanced products that facilitate language learning and the user experience. At the same time, Duolingo has been experiencing growing penetration rates on paid customers. Although Duolingo Max only has a penetration of 5% among paying customers, a higher price will improve KPIs in the mid-term, although margins will be affected in the short term due to important AI investments.

All of this while considering that the stock is undervalued and is not directly affected by tariffs. As a result, the stock has considerably outperformed the S&P 500 since March, by 10.23%. Taking all these factors into consideration, I will rate Duolingo's shares as a "buy."

data.title: Bank Buzz: Insider Buying And Share Repurchase Program Support Buy Rating For NB Bancorp
data.description:
Background

Bank Buzz covers the community bank sector, with a specific focus on mutual conversions, a niche within a niche.

It is an interesting hunting ground. Many of these little banks are inexpensive and possess multiple catalysts to increase shareholder value.

Our approach is to identify conversions trading below tangible book value [TBV] with overcapitalized balance sheets, solid asset quality, and shareholder-friendly management teams.

And then wait for leadership to unleash the catalysts.

Comp Table & Quick Takes

It's been a wild 4-5 months in the small-cap bank sector. The group enjoyed a strong ride in late '24/early '25 based on the prospects of a strong U.S. economy and a more active M&A environment.

However, most of the gains have been given back over the last couple of weeks as the tariff saga has created concerns of a potential U.S. recession.

Over the last six weeks, the movement across the sector has been quite interesting. Although most of the group is down double digits, a small handful has held on. First Seacoast (FSEA), ECB Bancorp (ECBK), and Texas Community (TCBS) are up 9%, 4%, and 2%, respectively.

As regular readers are aware, we tend to favor banks that are returning capital to shareholders via either dividends or share repurchases. Here's a quick update of notable announcements.

Recent Dividend Adjustments

  • Northeast Community Bancorp (NECB) declared a $0.20/share quarterly dividend, a 33.3% increase from the prior dividend of $0.15.
  • HarborOne (HONE) declared a $0.09/share quarterly dividend, a 12.5% increase from the prior dividend of $0.08.
  • Richmond Mutual (RMBI) declared a $0.15/share quarterly dividend, a 7.1% increase from the prior dividend of $0.14.

Recent Buyback Program Adoptions

  • NB Bancorp (NASDAQ:NBBK) announced its first post-conversion stock repurchase program for 5.0% of the shares outstanding.
  • TC Bancshares (OTC:TCBC) announced it has adopted its fourth post-conversion repurchase program for 5.0% of outstanding shares.

Given NBBK's buyback announcement and the stock's 18% decline over the last six weeks, we are updating our investment thesis.

NBBK: Insider Buying And Share Repurchase Program Support A Buy Rating

Introduction

NBBK is the holding company for Needham Bank. Founded in 1892, the bank is headquartered in Needham, MA, and serves the Greater Boston metropolitan area and surrounding communities, including eastern Connecticut, southern New Hampshire, and Rhode Island.

In December 2023, NBBK completed its mutual conversion, making it a fully public institution.

Investment Thesis

We are maintaining our buy rating on NBBK due to the following:

Attractive Portfolio: Asset quality is excellent, with a non-performing to total loan ratio of 0.3%. To put that into context, the average NPL/TL ratio for our coverage universe is more than double, or 0.7%.

Inexpensive Valuation: As illustrated in the comp table above, the stock trades at less than 90% of TBV ($17.89 per share). That's inexpensive, given the quality of the business. And insiders seem to agree, with several recent purchases (see table below) at higher values than Friday’s close of $16.00.

Robust Balance Sheet: With a tangible capital to asset (TC/A) ratio of almost 15%, NBBK possesses substantial liquidity to drive shareholder value via loan growth, dividends, and share repurchases. Given its attraction valuation, we would emphasize a buyback program and the leadership appears to agree. In 1Q, NBBK adopted its first post-conversion stock repurchase program targeting 5.0% of shares outstanding.

Shareholder/Employee Alignment: Insiders own about 2% of the bank and the Employee Stock Ownership Plan holds another 8% - everyone wins with a rising share price.

Valuation & Risks

Looking ahead over the next 2–4 years, we anticipate that management will adhere to the established, shareholder-friendly "thrift game plan," prioritizing conservative organic growth, dividends, prudent buybacks, and eventually, a sale.

In the past, thrifts have been acquired at an average of 130% of TBV. To remain conservative, we target an exit multiple of 120% of TBV.

For NBBK, we forecast TBV per share of ~$20 by the end of 2026, indicating an acquisition price of ~$24 per share or a ~50% return.

Primary Risks

Potential risks to our thesis include, but are not limited to:

  • The quality of the loan portfolio deteriorates. As a community bank, NBBK is significantly exposed to the economic conditions within its regional footprint.
  • Material change in the local competitive environment could slow growth or reduce profitability.
  • Leadership changes course and elects to hold excess capital (versus returning to shareholders via dividends and buybacks) or pursue a buy side acquisition.

Final Thoughts

Concerns of a U.S. recession in 2025 have reset small bank valuations. Over the last six weeks, the peer group's average return is -11%.

With an 18% decline during this period, we believe NBBK has been overly punished and like the risk/reward trading at about 89% of TBV.

data.title: Royal Caribbean: Solid Stock, But I Can't Unsee That Chart
data.description:
The good news: with stocks dropping like flies from high valuations and overvalued technicals, some are dropping into range for me. But at this point, I still see 20-40% downside risk in many of them. So closer to my buy area is not the same as buy.

Still, for those stocks I'm tracking to help build my long-term contrarian stock portfolio (hopefully sometime this year), it is great to see them getting "warmer." Royal Caribbean Cruises (NYSE:RCL), which I have been a customer of on several occasions (can I call that "research?"), has lost 1/3 of its peak value. Is it "cheap"? Some would say so, but I'm not analyzing the stock the way they are. And when a stock has appreciated 7x in under 2 years as RCL has, I'm focusing more energy on buying it in distressed times, rather than when a big segment of the market is still obsessed with "buying the dips."

My chart read = heresy?

This weekly chart tells me that regardless of the long-term fundamental advantages this stock has as an industry leader in a growth industry, there's still way too much risk in owning it. Ideally, I'd want to buy it at $40 a share because markets like the current one always put on the table that every "correction" will be followed by...several more corrections. But $140 would be a starting point to take a good look, unless the stock was showing earlier signs of pulling up and bottoming.

Momentum? Not so much

As I've written here many times, I am a big fan of Seeking Alpha's quant factor grades. But not all of them. As a career technician/price analyst, the momentum grade seems to be consistently late. Such is the case with a stock still earning a momentum grade of A-, despite falling by more than 30%.

I suspect that has something to do with moving average positioning, but I'll let someone else figure that out. I don't use it in my analysis, unless it is as a contrarian indicator. This is not new to me, I've always been skeptical of momentum as a factor. Or, as they say today, "number go up." It depends on where the number is going up, across multiple time frames.

Although an A- momentum isn't exactly what we're looking for, Royal Caribbean Cruises' Factor Grades surprised me very positively. The company gets an A in growth, profitability and revisions. The middle one (profitability) is the one I key in on, as my own quant and technical work takes over after I have convinced myself that a stock is buyable "at a price," even if that price is not available now.

Meanwhile, the valuation is a D-, but its forward price-to-earnings is 12x and forward EV-to-EBITDA is 10x, attractive multiples if the company is good and has solid prospects.

RCL Is A Solid Company

It turns out that this is the case with RCL. It's a very solid company, one of the largest in its industry, with very interesting fundamentals.

In the highlights below, you can see that the company delivered good margins, such as a 36% adj. EBITDA margin, an operating cash flow of $5.3 billion (which is quite interesting for a company with a market cap of $56 billion) and an adj. EPS of $11.80, with a growth of 74%.

Expectations are also very robust, with guidance of EPS of $14.35 to $14.65 for 2025 and estimates of continued double-digit growth for this indicator over the next few years.

With this excerpt from the earnings call, you can get a sense of Royal Caribbean Group's operational momentum and strategy:

"[...] With our industry-leading global brands, the most innovative fleet and private destinations and the best people, we remain focused on winning a greater share of the $2 trillion vacation market.

Our plan to capitalize on this opportunity continues to be grounded in our proven formula for success, moderate capacity growth, moderate yield growth, and strong cost control. [...]"

Valuation checks out

With that, RCL stock looks very interesting to have in the portfolio. It provides exposure to a good company and a low valuation that allows for attractive returns. For example, $14.50 EPS in 2025 is equivalent to an earnings yield of 7%.

The revenue chart below shows that even in an industry that is sensitive to macroeconomic factors, the company manages to present very stable and growing figures over time. However, the CapEx to sustain this is relatively high.

Therefore, when we compare net income with free cash flow, there is a considerable difference, so the 7% earnings yield doesn't reflect the company's full capacity to remunerate the shareholder, so the valuation isn't a huge bargain but reasonable (it's also not a D as shown in the factor grades).

Additionally, the company could see some impact of an economic slowdown due to tariffs. All in all, this is one for the watchlist, just not yet for the portfolio. All of the pre-checks are there for us. I just need to see that the sellers are not setting up to take the market much lower, and this stock with it.

data.title: Leggett & Platt: Restructuring And Selling, But Is It Enough?
data.description:
Leggett & Platt (NYSE:LEG) is a diversified US manufacturer of a range of engineered products and components, but perhaps best known for its bedding business. The business has a very long heritage, going back to the late 19th century, is active in a wide range of industries, yet has lost its way somewhat in more recent times.

Facing a dual problem of debt overhang and lack of competitiveness, Leggett has resorted to restructuring efforts, a massive dividend cut and a divestment as well. This now involves the sale of its aerospace activities, and while it will reduce debt, it will only make a dent in the leverage profile. Even as the business in theory could become a beneficiary of tariffs, I fail to have conviction on the proposition here.

A Diversified And Struggling Business

Leggett & Platt is quite a large business, with the company generating $4.4 billion in sales in 2024 split out across a number of industries. The largest of these is bedding, which includes components and finished products, ranging from full beds to mattress springs. This segment makes up 40% of sales.

Flooring & textiles and automotive each make up another 20% of sales with these activities including seat support, motors, cables, and the flooring products, including carpet cushions and furniture. Smaller, other applications include cylinders and aerospace.

The business is largely tied to the US, where it generates some 60% of sales, complemented by activities in China, Europe, Mexico and Canada.

While the scale and diversification look fine, Leggett faced some issues. This was already a $4 billion business in 2015 and grew sales to over $5 billion in 2021 and 2022. However, sales have fallen back to $4.4 billion, as more problematic is the fact that low double-digit operating margins have fallen to the mid single digits.

This has weighed heavily on the business and the shares, as a $50 stock in 2016 still traded at these levels in 2021, having fallen to lows of $6 per share here.

Zooming Into The Numbers

In February of this year, Leggett reported its 2024 results, and they were not pretty. Full-year sales were down 7% to $4.38 billion amidst a combination of falling prices and volumes. The company reported a big net loss of $511 million, equal to $3.73 per share, but this was largely the result of a massive impairment charge. Adjusted for this item, some more modest restructuring charges and one-offs, adjusted earnings were reported at $1.05 per share, this number being down from a $1.39 per share number in 2023.

Adjusted EBITDA for the year was down over a fifth to $402 million, worrisome as the company torches along quite a bit of debt, some $1.51 billion in net debt to be more precise, as net debt ratios come in a long way towards 4 times EBITDA. In fact, with 138 million shares outstanding, a $6 and change stock is valued at around $900 million here, less than its net debt load. This shows the extent of the challenges, as the existence of net debt and dismal operating results have created lingering pressures on the shares.

To address the issues, the company is reducing the footprint of activities and restructuring businesses, all designed to improve both the competitiveness of the business and to shore up the balance sheet. This looks nice, but is not yet seen in the results, as 2025 sales are seen down further to $4.0-$4.3 billion, down 2-9% amidst falling volume and pricing.

The company expects, or better said expected, to keep adjusted earnings flattish between $1.00 and $1.20 per share, which is the only silver lining, and badly needed. I deliberately said expected, as this outlook was given, of course, ahead of the recent turmoil rocking the economy and markets.

Making A Move

Trading at $6 and change, Leggett & Platt is valued around $2.4 billion here, at about 0.6 times sales. With the business facing both competitive and balance sheet challenges, the company has not just resorted to restructuring but divestment efforts as well.

In April, Leggett announced the sale of its aerospace products business to Tinicum Capital in a $285 million deal, although that after-tax proceeds are seen down to $240 million. The business provides engineered tube and duct assemblies, employs 700 workers and generates $190 million in sales, revealing that over a 1.2 times net sales multiple has been fetched. This looks good on paper, but the exact margins of the business are not known, as they are consolidated under the specialized products segments in the reporting.

The deal will reduce pro forma net debt to $1.27 billion, which is more comforting with a $402 million EBITDA number reported in 2024. Whilst aerospace makes up about 5% of sales, likely these are above-average activities in terms of profitability, as pro forma EBITDA might fall 10%. If that estimate is correct, a 3.8 times leverage ratio will come down to just 3.5 times, amidst the foregone earnings power as well. While this is positive, a 1.2 times sales multiple for an aerospace business looks relatively cheap, if we compare this to the wider industry.

This will provide some cash inflows, improve leverage a bit, but it also reduces the quality of the remaining business in my point of view, all while Leggett is entering this period of uncertainty with continued weakness, even if the company raises some cash and thereby reduces net debt, of course.

Expectations Are Reset

The latest deal makes perfect sense after last year was a year of reckoning for the business, when it did not just announce restructuring efforts, but the dividend was cut by approximately 90% as well, to still an annual payout of $0.20 per share. That was a very painful decision as the company has raised dividends for 50 years in a row before, as that move eliminated the track record in a single decision, and was a wake-up call that the business was no longer able to pursue the normal course.

That came as that previous dividend far superseded realistic earnings power, which given the dividend track record was arguably made too late, and was part of the reason why the company has been incurring quite a bit of net debt. Of course, much of this debt has been incurred following the purchase of Elite Comfort Solutions in a >$1 billion deal back in 2018.

Right now, the situation is highly fluid. While tariffs might actually benefit the business, as Leggett is highly focused on domestic production and suffers from cheap (Chinese) competition, the slowdown might also be a net negative, as the company is far from out of the woods here.

With the economic situation for a regular business being highly uncertain, the investment case of Leggett is furthermore complicated by a lack of competitiveness and debt overhang, all of which makes me very cautious about it being a hero here, not seeing a convincing reason to buy into the business just yet.

data.title: Budweiser Brewing: Management Changes And Price Hikes Are In The Limelight (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
My rating for Budweiser Brewing Company APAC (BDWBY) (BDWBF) (1876:HK) is changed to a Buy.

Earlier, I had a Neutral view of the stock after previewing its FY24 earnings in the March 8, 2024 write-up.

I'm now a bull on BDWBY, following an assessment of its new leadership and latest pricing moves.

China Division Should Realize Financial Improvement With Organizational Changes

BDWBF has a new CEO "Yanjun Cheng" or "YJC" in charge since the beginning of this month. The company also appointed "Fabio Sala" as its Mainland Chinese unit's president in 4Q24.

I think that its "APAC West" or "AW" segment (China operations) can achieve an improved set of results with a changed management team.

Budweiser was the worst-performer among its peers in the previous year.

BDWBY's topline fell by -9% for FY24. During the same period, China Resources Beer (OTCPK:CRHKY) (OTCPK:CRHKF) and Tsingtao Brewery (OTCPK:TSGTY) (OTCPK:TSGTF) suffered from relatively more modest revenue declines of -2% and -5%, respectively.

The company is the biggest player in the high-end segment of Asia's and the PRC's ("People's Republic Of China") alcoholic beverages market.

BDWBF's greater focus on expensive beer products means that its revenues have taken a substantial hit from China's economic slowdown. People in the country are going out less frequently as their purse strings tighten. Premium alcohol is mostly consumed in the on-trade channel like pubs. It's unsurprising that its AW unit's sales fell by -13% in 2024.

But a turnaround is on the cards.

Fabio Sala, who heads the company's Chinese business now, was previously involved with AB InBev's (BUD) European "in-home distribution" operations. This information was taken from Budweiser's 4Q24 analyst call ("S&P Capital IQ transcript") in late-Feb.

BDWBY is adopting a new approach for the AW division, and Sala's expertise will be useful. It plans to shift more resources to the off-trade channel, so as to offset on-premise weakness.

As per its fourth quarter earnings briefing commentary, the company is transitioning to a "new Budweiser Red color scheme" for product packaging and recruiting more "wholesalers". These are part of efforts to penetrate the off-trade category in the Chinese beer market. It wants to up-sell high-end beer to domestic consumers when they drink at home.

Separately, Seeking Alpha News reported in the middle of last month that BDWBF intends to cut "thousands of jobs" in response to the Chinese market's "weak consumer demand."

My take is that this latest development is reflective of the new CEO's past experience. YJC is a Chinese citizen unlike his predecessor, and he managed supply chain-related matters for the past one and a half decades.

It's highly probable that Yanjun will place his emphasis on enhancing the operating efficiency of the company's PRC operations in the future. This seems to be the case considering recent news flow.

My forecasts imply that its AW segment will register a positive +1% topline expansion and stable EBITDA margin of around 28% in 2025. This contrasts with this business' -1.7 ppts ("percentage points") EBITDA margin contraction and sales drop last year.

Price Increases Have Positive Read-throughs For Korean Unit's Prospects

South Korean ("SK") media JoongAng published a news article on March 30 noting that BDWBY has initiated a +3% "hike" for its beverage items in April. This follows on from the earlier 8% increase in selling prices for the company's selected high-end beers late last year.

The operating entity for BDWBF's operations in Korea is "Oriental Brewery" carrying the "Cass" brand. Its performance is reported under the company's other segment, "APAC East" or "AE".

My view is that these actions send a clear message regarding its effective product strategy and the favorable pricing structure in SK.

BDWBY has adapted well to evolving consumer trends in Korea. The people in this nation are becoming more mindful of staying healthy. The company has tweaked its product portfolio to meet such needs.

The sales of both its non-alcoholic "Cass 0.0" and sugar-free "Cass Light" beers expanded by more than +30% in SK for FY2024. The popularity of these items has granted substantial pricing power to BDWBF.

Also, there is scope for further upward adjustments, as the Korean alcohol sector has yet to exploit its full potential.

Budweiser shared at its Q4 results call that the "price and margin ladder in Korea is much more compressed" vis-à-vis that for other countries. This implies that BDWBY has the ability to charge a heftier premium for its high-quality beers using industry benchmarks in markets outside SK.

I'm predicting a high-single digit percentage growth in revenue and a +200 bps ("basis points") EBITDA-to-sales improvement for AE.

You Should Think About These Bear-Case Scenarios

My bull-case thesis for Budweiser could be broken under specific situations. BDWBF might fail to increase its penetration of China's at-home channel in a meaningful way. Korean consumers can possibly switch to its competitors which offer similar beers at lower prices.

My Conclusion

I am upgrading BDWBY to a Buy.

My opinion is that the stock's earnings multiple can re-rate from the current 17 times to its historical three-year mean of 24x. This points to a capital appreciation potential of +41%.

CRHKY and TSGTY are now trading at similar mid-to-high teens P/E ratios of 15.6 and 16.6, respectively as per S&P Capital IQ data.

BDWBF should be able to command a higher valuation than its peers by virtue of its leadership in the high-end segment. Further success in SK and the expansion of the Chinese in-home segment will serve as multiple expansion triggers for the company's shares.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Basis Trade Sent Yields Soaring - Is It A Warning?
data.description:
Tariff Reprieve Sends Stocks Surging

Last week, we noted that the market was not expecting retaliation from China.

“Rather than coming to the table to negotiate, China responded with a reciprocal 34% tariff on the U.S. plus export controls on rare earth metals needed for technological production. China is playing “hardball” negotiating tactics with Trump. This was a smart move from a negotiating standpoint by China, allowing President Xi to open tariff discussions from a point of strength. However, without some resolution to the extraordinary tariffs, the market will remain in turmoil for quite some time.”

That battle persisted this week as Trump raised tariffs on China to 104%, and China then retaliated with a further tariff increase of 84%. However, as we said last week, any good news would cause the market to rally sharply. On Wednesday, President Trump announced a 90-pause on the full effect of new tariffs. Interestingly, the same headline sent stocks surging on Monday but was quickly deemed “fake news” by the White House. I suspect that Monday was a “leak” by the White House to test the market response, and President Trump kept that announcement handly to stave off a further decline in the markets. Whatever the reason, the markets needed the break. Here is Trump’s full statement:

From a technical view, the market completed an expected retracement from the October 2022 lows. Last week, we laid out the potential correction levels.

  1. The recent lows are around 5500. (That level was violated)
  2. Immediately below that is the 38.2% retracement level at 5134 (Is being challenged)
  3. Lastly, the 50% retracement level at 4816 should hold, barring the onset of a fiscal event or recession.

Notably, we stated that:

“The market should be able to find some support at this level and muster a short-term rally next week. However, there is a downside risk to 4816, which would be a 50% retracement of the bull market rally. Any positive announcements over the weekend could spark a relatively robust reversal rally, given the more than three-standard deviation gap between where the market closed and the 50-DMA.“

That 38.2% retracement level, using the bull market from October 2022 lows, was broken early Monday morning as stocks plunged lower amid rising tariff concerns and a blowup in the bond market. However, the market finally tested the 50% retracement level on Wednesday morning. Given the deep oversold condition, President Trump’s announcement to pause tariffs led to the 3rd largest single-day rally since WWII. For now, the market should be able to hold support at the previous lows and hopefully find a bit more relief into next week.

As I noted in the previous two weeks, we strongly lean toward the potential of the markets beginning a more extensive corrective process, much like in 2022. We will revisit that analysis in this weekend’s newsletter. However, while we are concerned about a continued correction process as markets realign prices to forward earnings expectations, there will still be strong intermittent rallies. As noted last week, nothing in the market is guaranteed. Therefore, we continue managing risk accordingly, and as we stated last week and executed on Wednesday, we are now in “sell the rally” mode until the markets find equilibrium. When that will be, we are uncertain, so we continue to watch the technicals, make small moves within portfolios, and reduce volatility risk as needed.

This week, we will revisit the 2022 scenario and the “basis trade,” which threatens the financial markets.

Credit Spreads Sending A Warning

As discussed in “Credit Spreads,” the one market signal worth your attention is “credit.”

Credit spreads reflect the perceived risk of corporate bonds compared to government bonds. The spread between risky corporate bonds and safer Treasury bonds remains narrow when the economy performs well. This is because investors are confident in corporate profitability and are willing to accept lower yields for higher risks. Conversely, during economic uncertainty or stress, investors demand higher yields for holding corporate debt, causing spreads to widen. This widening often signals investors are growingly concerned about future corporate defaults, which could indicate broader economic trouble.

The two charts above show that credit spreads are essential for stock market investors. Watching spreads provide insights into the health of the corporate sector, which is a major driver of equity performance. When credit spreads widen, they often precede liquidity events, reduced corporate earnings, economic contractions, and stock market downturns. Widening credit spreads are commonly associated with increased risk aversion among investors. Historically, significant widening of credit spreads has foreshadowed recessions and major market sell-offs. Here’s why:

  1. Corporate Financial Health: Credit spreads reflect investor views on corporate solvency. A rising spread suggests a growing concern over companies’ ability to service their debt. Particularly if the economy slows or interest rates rise.
  2. Risk Sentiment Shift: Credit markets tend to be more sensitive to economic shocks than equity markets. When credit spreads widen, it typically indicates that the fixed-income market is pricing in higher risks. This is often a leading indicator of equity market stress.
  3. Liquidity Events: As investors become more risk-averse, they shift capital from corporate bonds to safer assets like Treasuries. The flight to safety reduces liquidity in the corporate bond market. Less liquidity potentially leads to tighter credit conditions that affect businesses’ ability to invest and grow, weighing on stock prices.

The recent market disruption caused by Trump’s trade war has undoubtedly widened spreads between “risk-free” treasury yields and corporate bonds. However, while those spreads have widened, they remain well below the long-term averages. With inflation and economic growth slowing, this week’s violent turmoil in the Treasury bond market is a signal of more than just recession concerns. As we noted in our Daily Market Commentary:

“On Monday, Treasury bonds had a sharp decline far beyond what the economic or tariff data suggested would be the case. We suspect that on Monday, there was forced liquidation through either margin calls or demand redemption of an institutional fund. The outsized selling and volume on a single day for bonds is highly unusual. The media excuses of “tariffs” or “economic concerns” are issues the bond market has known about for quite some time.”

That type of sharp liquidation has historically been the issue of some liquidity events in the bond market. In this case, it appears to be the heavily leveraged arbitrage trade used by hedge funds.

The “Basis Trade” – A Near LTCM Moment

For a simple explanation of the basis trade, I asked Grok:

“The “basis trade” in the Treasury bond market is a strategy commonly used by hedge funds to profit from small price discrepancies between Treasury bonds (the cash market) and Treasury futures contracts. Here’s how it works: hedge funds buy Treasury bonds while simultaneously shorting Treasury futures (or vice versa), betting that the price gap, or “basis,” between the two will converge. This trade relies heavily on leverage—often 20 to 56 times the initial investment—borrowed through the repurchase (repo) market, where Treasuries are used as collateral. The goal is to capture small, consistent profits from this arbitrage, amplified by the leverage while assuming the relationship between cash bonds and futures remains stable.”

While the Fed suggests that the hedge fund basis trades leverage is above 50x, the Treasury Borrowing Advisory Committee wrote in January that “20x appears to be a good approximation of leverage typically used in these trades.”

If you don’t understand that math, it simply means that a 5% loss at 20x leverage is a 100% loss on the trade.

Furthermore, just for context, when Long Term Capital Management (LTCM) blew up over an unexpected move in interest rates, it cost the Fed $100 billion to keep financial markets afloat. As shown below, the current magnitude of today’s hedge fund giants running these “basis trades” are multiples of LTCM.

As of this past week, the market liquidity event appears to be the “basis trade” unraveling. This turbulence is driven by hedge funds being forced to unwind their basis trade positions as Treasury prices drop (and yields rise.) That change in the value of the collateral backing those leveraged bets decreases, triggering margin calls from lenders. To meet those margin calls, hedge funds must sell their Treasury holdings, which exacerbates the downward pressure on bond prices, increasing yields, which triggers more liquidations.

This “liquidation event” in the bond market is very reminiscent of the “Repo Crisis” in 2019 and the “Dash For Cash” during the COVID-19 pandemic. It also reminds us of the “Silicon Valley Bank Crisis” in 2023. In all three events, the Federal Reserve stepped in to provide liquidity to the Treasury market. As Deutsche Bank noted on Wednesday:

“As far as the market circuit-breakers go, if recent disruption in the US Treasury market continues we see no other option for the Fed but to step in with emergency purchases of US Treasuries to stabilize the bond market (“emergency QE”). This would be very similar to the Bank of England intervention following the gilt crisis of 2022. While we suspect the Fed could be successful in stabilizing the market in the short-term, we would argue there is only one thing that can stabilize some of the more medium-term financial market shifts that have been unleashed: a reversal in the policies of the Trump administration itself.”

The reversal of Trump’s tariffs on Wednesday was not a “master class in negotiation.” It was the admission that a terrible mistake was made, and the bond market drove that reversal.

However, is the problem resolved? It does not appear to be due to yields continuing to rise. Both credit spreads and interest rates are sending warning signs but are not yet at levels suggesting the entire funding market is broken. The bond market is functioning, and the 10-year auction on Wednesday went well. Furthermore, hopefully, the administration’s 90-day pause on tariffs is the first step in the right direction to end the trade war entirely or at least reduce it significantly.

Regardless, I doubt that we are done with market volatility. The damage done to the bond market so far is likely not over yet, and while we may get a short-term reprieve, I suspect there will be another round of bond market stress before we are done.

That is particularly the case given the recent triggering of our most crucial risk-management signal.

Despite The Tariff Reprieve – Sell Signal Triggers

Last Tuesday, I posted “Failure At The 200-DMA” which discussed the importance of the weekly “risk management” signal.

The chart below is a long-term weekly chart of RSI and MACD indicators. I have denoted when the indicators are trading in bullish and bearish trends. The primary signal is the crossover of the weekly moving averages, as noted by the vertical lines. While the MACD and RSI indicators provided early warning signals, the moving average crossover confirmed a market correction or consolidation. These indicators will not necessarily cause a risk reduction precisely at the top. However, they generally provide sufficient indications to reduce risk ahead of more significant market corrections and consolidations.

I have updated the chart for this week’s market close. The massive rally on Wednesday completed the 50% retracement from the low, which allowed us to reduce portfolio risk in portfolios. Most likely, the correction process that started with the break of the 200-DMA is likely not yet complete. We will discuss those actions in the “How We Are Trading It” section below.

The market tells us that the risk of a more significant correction or consolidation process is increasing, particularly as economic growth slows and valuations are repriced for reduced earnings growth expectations. As we noted in that article two weeks ago:

“While such does not preclude a significant counter-trend rally in the short term, the longer-term risks seem to be growing.”

So far, the current corrective cycle, including the massive reflexive rally this week, remains very reminiscent of the 2022 correction. If we enter another corrective period like 2022, given some of the same technical similarities, there is a decent “playbook” to follow despite substantial differences. In 2022, the Fed was hiking rates, inflation was surging, and economists were convinced a recession was on the horizon. As noted above, earnings estimates were revised lower, causing the markets to reprice valuations. Today, the Fed is cutting rates, and inflation is declining; however, due to Trump’s trade policies, the risk of recession is rising, and earnings estimates likely remain overly optimistic. We must realize that the analysis can change as time passes.

However, let’s review the 2022 correction process. In March 2022, the market triggered the weekly “sell signal” as it declined. Notably, the market rallied sharply after the “sell signal” was initially triggered. Much to the same degree as we saw on Wednesday. Such a rally is unsurprising, as when markets trigger “sell signals,” they are often profoundly oversold in the short term. However, that rally was an opportunity to “reduce risk,” as the failure of that rally brought sellers back into the market. The “decline, rally, decline” process repeated until the market bottomed in October. One of the defining things we will be looking to identify where the current market will bottom is the positive divergence of momentum and relative strength. Even though markets continued to struggle in the summer or 2022, the positive divergences suggested that market lows were near.

In that article two weeks ago, we stated:

“With the market approaching decently oversold levels, I expect a rally to start as soon as this week or next.”

That rally occurred, and we are starting to track the initial “sell signal” process as of 2022. Such suggests we could see a further rally over the next week.

We used the rally this week to reduce portfolio risk and raise cash levels, and we will continue that process on a further rally. While no two corrections are the same, it is essential to understand that corrections do not occur in a straight line. With the weekly sell signal in place, investors should expect that we will likely see further declines, which will likely be punctuated by short-term rallies that allow investors to rebalance portfolio allocations and reduce risk as needed.

With that understanding, this is what we did this past week.

How We Are Trading It

Last week, we stated:

“As noted, we expect a sizable rally soon. While such a rally will undoubtedly make investors more bullish on the markets, we will use that rally to reduce portfolio volatility until a more durable market bottom is identified. Furthermore, we are triggering an important weekly sell signal. Still, the markets are simultaneously three standard deviations below their longer-term moving average, challenging the rising trend line from the October 2022 lows. Such oversold conditions typically precede short-term rallies, allowing us to reduce exposure to equities between 5500 and 5700. While the rally could be more significant, we will use those levels to begin risk reductions.”

We followed our instructions from last week, which stated that we would:

  1. Reduce current positions by 1/4 to 1/2 of their current target weights.
  2. Increase cash levels
  3. Raise stop loss levels on long-term positions.
  4. Sell positions that have technically violated previous support levels or exceeded risk tolerances.
  5. Add to positions that are positioned to benefit from further market stress.

All the trades are listed at the bottom of this week’s newsletter.

Those actions reduced equity exposure to 50% and increased cash to 17%. However, the portfolio has a 2.5% short-S&P 500 position, which brings the actual equity allocation to 47.5%.

Note: We expect the market to rally more from current levels over the next week or so and could challenge the 200-DMA. However, that is likely the point where sellers will re-enter the market. Markets rarely bottom without retracing toward the previous lows or setting new lows. Given the technical damage to the market, we suspect we will see a pullback before this correction process is over.

We will continue to monitor and adjust the portfolio accordingly. At some point, the valuation reversion will be complete, allowing us to reconfigure portfolio allocations for a more bullish environment. When that event occurs, we will reduce fixed income, shift income allocations to corporate bonds rather than government bonds, and overweight equity allocations in portfolios.

However, that is a conversation we will have in more detail in the future. For now, market conditions remain uncertain. Preparing and adjusting strategies can help investors navigate volatility confidently. As technical indicators flash warning signs, a well-structured risk management approach will protect capital and preserve long-term gains.

I hope this helps.

SimpleVisor Top & Bottom Performers By Sector

S&P 500 Weekly Tear Sheet

Relative Performance Analysis

Stocks had another very volatile week, with the bond market getting crushed as forced selling erupted over the “basis trade.” Such was quite the reversal from last week when bonds were the shining star of the markets. Nonetheless, as we stated last week:

“Given the extremely deep oversold of every sector and market, I would expect a tradeable rally early next week, barring any more antics from the White House. As you will note in our Risk/Reward ranges below, while markets and sectors are well outside of normal tolerances, suggesting a rally is likely, the number of bearish divergences will keep markets contained for quite some time. Use rallies to reduce risk and rebalance exposures as needed.”

That remains the case this week. More upside is likely as the markets are not overbought yet, but we will want to continue to rebalance and reduce equity risk on that rally as needed.

Technical Composite

The technical overbought/sold gauge comprises several price indicators (R.S.I., Williams %R, etc.), measured using “weekly” closing price data. Readings above “80” are considered overbought, and below “20” are oversold. The market peaks when those readings are 80 or above, suggesting prudent profit-taking and risk management. The best buying opportunities exist when those readings are 20 or below.

The current reading is 27.88 out of a possible 100.

Portfolio Positioning “Fear / Greed” Gauge

The “Fear/Greed” gauge is how individual and professional investors are “positioning” themselves in the market based on their equity exposure. From a contrarian position, the higher the allocation to equities, the more likely the market is closer to a correction than not. The gauge uses weekly closing data.

NOTE: The Fear/Greed Index measures risk from 0 to 100. It is a rarity that it reaches levels above 90.

The current reading is 28.80 out of a possible 100.

Relative Sector Analysis

Most Oversold Sector Analysis

Sector Model Analysis & Risk Ranges

How To Read This Table

  • The table compares the relative performance of each sector and market to the S&P 500 index.
  • “MA XVER” (Moving Average Crossover) is determined by the short-term weekly moving average crossing positively or negatively with the long-term weekly moving average.
  • The risk range is a function of the month-end closing price and the “beta” of the sector or market. (Ranges reset on the 1st of each month)
  • The table shows the price deviation above and below the weekly moving averages.

As noted last week:

“The crash this past week sent every market and sector below normal monthly risk ranges. The only other time I have seen something like this was during the 2020 pandemic shutdown of the economy. Notably, these deviations provide opportunistic entry points for investors in the short term. More opportunities to add exposure now reside in Financials, Technology, Discretionary, and Communications. Take profits in Bonds after their recent run higher.“

That advice worked, as bonds crashed and stocks made a decent move higher off their lows. The situation is reversing, with bonds very oversold and equities moving higher. Use the rally to reduce equity risk as needed, and watch the bond market for signs the “basis trade” is rectifying itself. There is a very good opportunity to add long-duration bonds to the portfolio coming soon.

Weekly SimpleVisor Stock Screens

We provide three stock screens each week from SimpleVisor.

This week, we are searching for the Top 20:

  • Relative Strength Stocks
  • Momentum Stocks
  • Technical Strength W/ Dividends

RSI Screen

Momentum Screen

Technically Strong With Dividends

SimpleVisor Portfolio Changes

We post all of our portfolio changes as they occur at SimpleVisor:

Apr 9th

However, when this signal is triggered, markets tend to be very oversold and set up for a near-term rally where we can reduce risk at a better level. At the same time, a more critical liquidity event is brewing in the bond market.

The recent spike in bond yields starting Monday is a forced liquidation event in the bond market. It suggests rising credit risk, particularly in hedge funds’ highly leveraged arbitrage trades. In 2019 and 2020, we saw similar events where the Federal Reserve was forced to step in and become a “buyer of last resort.” While that hasn’t happened yet, if rates keep rising as sharply as they have been, the Fed will be forced to maintain financial stability in the credit markets. As such, we are shortening our bond duration sharply until this crisis passes and shifting exposure from stocks most impacted by credit risk increases.

Once this crisis is over, we will rebalance portfolios and increase duration to take advantage of slower economic growth and a potential recession.”

That rally came yesterday, and we are reducing risk across all models this morning. The first step is to reduce exposure to positions, reduce target weights, and increase cash levels.
